entry.title: End to End Guide: Enabling SAP Joule in SAP S/4HANA Fiori Launchpad (PCE + OnPremise PoC)
entry.content:
1. Introduction

SAP Joule is SAP's generative AI, designed to help users accomplish tasks through natural language interaction within SAP applications. This guide will provide a step by step walkthrough to enable joule in SAP Fiori.

The guide is primarily written for SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Private Edition (PCE).
PS: Also covers steps that differ for On-Premise S/4HANA (for a Proof-of-Concept environment).

2. Component Overview

Before starting, it is important to understand the key components involved and the role each plays.

Component

What It Is

Role in This Integration

SAP Joule

SAP's generative AI copilot. A cloud service on SAP BTP.

The AI assistant end users interact with. Loaded in FLP header as a Shell Plugin.

SAP BTP

SAP Business Technology Platform  the cloud foundation.

Hosts Joule, Work Zone, Identity Services, and destinations connecting to S/4HANA.

SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition

SAP's digital workplace platform. Can serve as launchpad or content bridge.

Acts as the technical bridge between Joule and S/4HANA. Required in the Joule Formation.

SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS)

SAP's Identity Authentication Service. Manages user identity and SSO.

Provides the SSO layer. All BTP services require IAS authentication.

SAP Cloud Connector (SCC)

An on-premise agent creating a secure tunnel from BTP to on-premise systems.

Enables Work Zone and Joule on BTP to securely reach S/4HANA.

SAP Fiori Launchpad (FLP)

The S/4HANA web UI hub where users launch Fiori apps.

The surface where the Joule icon appears via a Shell Plugin target mapping.

Formation

A BTP System Landscape construct grouping systems for a specific integration.

Tells Joule which Work Zone, IAS tenant, and S/4HANA belong together.

CDM3

Common Data Model v3 . SAP Fiori's content model (catalogs, tiles, groups).

Used to expose S/4HANA Fiori content to BTP so Work Zone can sync it.

3. Architecture Overview

The following describes the end-to-end integration flow:

  • End User opens S/4HANA Fiori Launchpad in browser
  • FLP authenticates via IAS (after SSO is configured)
  • FLP loads the Joule Shell Plugin from the Joule Web Client (sapdas.cloud.sap)
  • Joule on BTP connects to Work Zone via the Joule Formation
  • Work Zone reaches S/4HANA via BTP Destinations → Cloud Connector → ABAP Server(S/4 system)
  • Joule icon appears in FLP header. Users interact with Joule through natural language.

4. Prerequisites

Requirement

Details

SAP BTP Global Account

Admin access. Subaccount in the same region as your IAS tenant.

SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS)

IAS tenant associated with your BTP Global Account.

SAP S/4HANA System

PCE or On-Premise (for PoC). FLP accessible via browser.

SAP Cloud Connector

Installed and running. Registered to your BTP subaccount.

S/4HANA Technical User

User with access to CDM3 service (/sap/bc/ui2/cdm3) and FLP admin roles.

BTP Entitlements

Joule (foundation), SAP Build Work Zone standard edition, SAP Cloud Identity Services.

Regional Alignment

BTP subaccount, IAS tenant, and Work Zone must be in the same region.

Note: PCE vs On-Premise: PCE uses token-based registration via Communication Arrangements. On-Premise uses Cloud Connector and manual destinations. Each step calls out differences.

5. Step-by-Step Guide

Step 1: Enable Cloud Foundry in BTP Subaccount

Why: Cloud Foundry is the runtime where BTP service instances (Work Zone, Joule) are deployed. Without it, subscriptions and the Joule booster cannot run.

  1. Open SAP BTP Cockpit → navigate to your target Subaccount → Overview.
  2. Click Enable Cloud Foundry. Accept defaults (Plan: standard). Click Create.
  3. Once enabled, click Create Space → name it e.g. 'dev'. Assign yourself Space Developer + Space Manager roles.

Validation: Subaccount Overview shows CF Enabled, Org Name, API Endpoint, and space 'dev' listed.

On-Premise PoC: No difference. This step is identical for both PCE and On-Premise.

 

Step 2: Add Entitlements

Why: Entitlements grant your subaccount the right to use specific BTP services. Without them, the services cannot be subscribed to or instantiated.

  1. Go to Global Account → Entitlements → Entity Assignments.
  2. Select your subaccount → Configure Entitlements → Add Service Plans.
  3. Add: Joule (also search as 'das-application') — plan: foundation
  4. Add: SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition — plan: standard
  5. Add: SAP Cloud Identity Services — plan: connectivity
  6. Click Save.

Validation: All three services appear in Entitlements list with quota assigned.

Step 3: Establish Trust to IAS

Why: All BTP services (including Joule) authenticate via IAS. Establishing trust allows your subaccount to delegate authentication to IAS, enabling SSO across all connected services.

  1. Go to BTP Subaccount → Security → Trust Configuration.
  2. Click Establish Trust.
  3. Select your IAS tenant (e.g., yourtenantid.accounts.ondemand.com).
  4. Click Next through the wizard → Finish.

Validation: Trust Configuration shows your IAS tenant with status Active.

Step 4: Configure Trusted Domains in BTP

Why: BTP Trusted Domains control which browser origins can interact with your subaccount's services via iFrames and cross-origin calls. Without this, the Joule panel cannot be embedded in your FLP.

  1. Go to BTP Subaccount → Security → Settings → Trusted Domains.
  2. Click Add for each of the following origins (scheme + host + port if non-standard):
  • Your FLP origin: e.g. https://your-s4-host:44301
  • Your Joule Web Client: https://<tenant>.<region>.sapdas.cloud.sap
  • Your Work Zone site: https://<site-id>.launchpad.cfapps.<region>.hana.ondemand.com
  1. Click Save.

Note: Your Joule URL is found in System Landscape → Formations → your Joule formation → URL column of the Joule system entry.(You can maintain it later once Joule is activated via booster etc.)

Validation: All required origins appear in the Trusted Domains list.

Step 5: Configure Trusted Domains in IAS

Why: IAS maintains its own trusted domain allowlist. Cross-domain authentication requires IAS to trust the same origins as BTP.

  1. Open IAS Admin Console: https://<ias-tenant>.accounts.ondemand.com/admin
  2. Go to Applications & Resources → Tenant Settings → Customization → Trusted Domains.
  3. Click Add — enter hostname only (no scheme or port) for each host.
  4. Click Save.

Note: Format difference: BTP accepts full origins (https://host:44301). IAS accepts hostname only (host.example.com).

Validation: IAS shows 'Trusted Domains updated' confirmation.

Step 6: Expose SAP Fiori Launchpad Content (CDM3)

Why: Work Zone needs to know what Fiori apps and catalogs exist in S/4HANA. The CDM3 export makes this content discoverable so Work Zone can sync it.

Pre-requisite: Activate CDM3 ICF Service

  1. In S/4HANA, run transaction SICF.
  2. Navigate to /sap/bc/ui2/cdm3. If inactive, right-click → Activate Service.

Expose Content

  1. Run transaction /n/UI2/CDM3_EXP_SCOPE.
  2. Select Version 1.
  3. Choose All roles (first-time setup) or select specific roles.
  4. Click Expose Content in the toolbar. Wait for completion.
  5. Click View Log to verify results.

Note: Warnings in the CDM3 log are normal. Red errors should be investigated but typically do not block the integration.

Validation: Log shows entities exposed (e.g., '14,663 out of 14,671 entities exposed'). Click View Exposed Content to confirm.

Step 7: Set Up SAP Cloud Connector

Why: Cloud Connector creates a secure outbound-only tunnel from your on-premise/PCE network to BTP. Work Zone and Joule use this tunnel to reach S/4HANA without inbound firewall rules.

Please setup cloud connector first.

7.1 Register BTP Subaccount

  1. Open SCC Admin UI (e.g. https://localhost:8443).
  2. Add your BTP subaccount using Download Authentication Data from BTP → Connectivity → Cloud Connectors.
  3. Confirm status shows Connected (green).

7.2 Create Back-End Mapping

  1. Go to Cloud to On-Premises → + Add:
  • Protocol: HTTPS (preferred) or HTTP (PoC)
  • Internal Host/Port: e.g. S/4HANA internal hostname + HTTPS port 44301 or HTTP port 8001
  • Virtual Host: a clean alias (e.g., s4hana-virtual)
  • Back-End Type: ABAP System
  1. Add Resources: /sap/bc/ui2/cdm3, /sap/bc/ui2/flp, /sap/bc/ui5_ui5 — all with 'Path and All Sub-Paths'.

Validation: Mapping shows green 'Reachable' status. BTP Cockpit → Connectivity → Cloud Connectors shows 'Available'.

On-Premise PoC: If HTTPS port 44301 is blocked between SCC and ABAP server, use HTTP port 8001 for PoC. In SICF, navigate to /sap/bc/ui2/cdm3 → Logon Data tab → check 'Use all logon procedures' to allow Basic Authentication.

Step 8: Activate Subscriptions and Instances

Why: Subscriptions activate the actual services in your subaccount. Without subscribing to Work Zone and Joule, neither the Joule icon nor the content bridge can function.

  1. Go to BTP Subaccount → Instances and Subscriptions → Create.
  2. Subscribe to SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition — plan: standard.
  3. Subscribe to Joule (das-application) — plan: foundation.
  4. For Work Zone: also create a Service Instance (name: BuildZone) and a Service Key.
  5. Launch Work Zone from Instances & Subscriptions at least once — this registers it in System Landscape.

Note: The Joule Booster (next step) looks for the Work Zone subscription in its dropdown. It only appears after you launch Work Zone at least once.

Validation: Subscriptions show 'Subscribed'. Service Key created. System Landscape → Systems shows Work Zone auto-registered.

Step 9: Configure the Joule Formation

Why: A Formation is a logical grouping that tells Joule which Work Zone, IAS tenant, and S/4HANA belong together. Without a formation, Joule has no context about which systems to connect to.

Option A — Run the Joule Booster (Recommended for PCE)

  1. Go to Global Account → Boosters → search 'Setting Up Joule' → click Start.
  2. Wizard: Select subaccount and CF space (dev).
  3. Step 3 — Select Integrations: choose SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition.
  4. Step 4 — Select Capabilities: SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition + SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition.
  5. Step 5 — Select your Work Zone system and S/4HANA system.
  6. Click Execute and wait for completion.

Option B — Add Systems to Existing Formation

  1. Go to Global Account → System Landscape → Formations.
  2. Find the formation of type 'Integration with Joule' with status Ready.
  3. Click Include Systems → add your Work Zone instance and S/4HANA system.
  4. Confirm integration options (Enable Capability Deployment, Enable Joule Icon).

Validation: Formation shows status Ready (green). Includes IAS, Work Zone, and Joule systems. Note the Joule URL — needed for Trusted Domains and shell plugin.

On-Premise PoC: On-Premise systems cannot complete Communication Arrangement token registration.

Recommended approach:

(1) Run the booster selecting only Work Zone as the integration (not S/4HANA).
(2) Register S/4HANA manually in System Landscape → Systems → Add → Type: SAP S/4HANA Cloud. (Not needed for POC)

Step 10: Configure BTP Destinations

Why: BTP Destinations define how Work Zone and Joule connect to S/4HANA. Without correct destinations, Work Zone cannot read content and Joule cannot reach the backend.

Create the following destination (Design-Time and Runtime can be the same for PoC):

Property

Value

Name

e.g, jouletc  (lowercase, no underscores)

Type

HTTP

URL

http://<scc-virtual-host>:<port>/sap/bc/ui2/cdm3

Proxy Type

OnPremise

Authentication

BasicAuthentication

User / Password

S/4HANA technical user with CDM3 access

sap-client

Your S/4HANA client (e.g., 100)

HTML5.DynamicDestination

true

WebIDEEnabled

true

WebIDEUsage

odata_abap

 

Critical: Work Zone Destination Naming: Work Zone only accepts destination names that are lowercase with no underscores. Names like MCF_TC will cause an error. Use names like jouletc or s4hana.

Critical: URL Path: The destination URL must include the CDM3 path: /sap/bc/ui2/cdm3. Without this, Work Zone receives an HTML login page instead of JSON, resulting in an authorization error.

Validation: Check Connection returns HTTP 200. e.g. SWZ_JOULE destination is auto-created and visible in the destinations list.

 

Step 11: Configure Work Zone Channel Manager

Why: The Channel Manager connects Work Zone to S/4HANA as a Content Provider. This sync imports S/4HANA's Fiori catalogs and makes the app landscape available to Joule.

  1. Launch SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition from Instances & Subscriptions.
  2. Go to Channel Manager → + New.
  3. Fill in:
  • Title: your system name (e.g., S4H_UIB)
  • Design-Time Destination: your lowercase destination (e.g., jouletc)
  • Runtime Destination: same
  • Content Scope: Roles and related content
  • Automatically add all content items: ON
  1. Click Save, then click the sync icon.

Wait 5-15 minutes for first sync

On-Premise PoC: The channel may show 'Updated' with a 'Connectivity Error' in the Report. This error is for real-time change notification callbacks, which on-premise systems do not support. The content sync itself succeeds. Re-sync manually when S/4HANA content changes.

Validation: Channel shows Updated status. Content Manager → Explore confirms S/4HANA catalogs are visible.

Step 12: Configure Joule Shell Plugin in S/4HANA FLP

Why: The Joule icon in FLP is delivered via a SAPUI5 Shell Plugin configured as a Target Mapping in an FLP catalog. Without this step, users will not see the Joule icon regardless of BTP configuration.

12.1 Create Technical Catalog

  1. In S/4HANA, run transaction /n/UI2/FLPD_CUST (Launchpad Content Manager: Cross-Client).
  2. In the Catalogs tab, create a new catalog: ID = Z_JOULE_TECH, Title = 'Joule Technical Catalog'. Package: $TMP (PoC).

12.2 Create Shell Plugin Target Mapping

  1. Open catalog Z_JOULE_TECH in the Launchpad Designer (opens in browser).
  2. Click Target Map... tab → Create Target Mapping.
  3. Fill in:
  • Semantic Object: Shell
  • Action: plugin  (type manually — not in dropdown)
  • Application Type: SAPUI5 Fiori App
  • URL: https://<joule-tenant>.<region>.sapdas.cloud.sap/resources/public/webclient/s4  (NO trailing slash)
  • Title: Joule
  • ID: sap.das.webclientplugin.s4
  • Save.

Critical: No Trailing Slash: The Joule Web Client URL must NOT end with a slash. Use .../webclient/s4 not .../webclient/s4/. A trailing slash causes 'File not found' and the icon will not appear.

Note: How to find your Joule URL: Go to System Landscape → Formations → your Joule formation. The Joule system shows a URL like https://<tenant>.eu10.sapdas.cloud.sap. Append /resources/public/webclient/s4 to this base URL.

12.3 Create Role and Assign Catalog

  1. Run transaction PFCG → create single role Z_JOULE_S4HANA.
  2. Menu tab → click Launchpad Catalog → assign Z_JOULE_TECH (Local Front-End Server).
  3. Authorizations tab → Change Authorization Data → Do not select templates → Generate → Save.
  4. User tab → assign role to target users → User Comparison → Full Comparison → OK.

Validation: Log in to FLP. The Joule icon (sparkle/diamond) appears in the FLP header. Clicking it opens the Joule panel.

Step 13: Configure IAS SSO for S/4HANA FLP (Recommended)

Why: Joule is secured by IAS. If FLP uses ABAP local login, users must log in again when Joule opens. Configuring IAS SSO for FLP gives a seamless single login experience.

13.1 Download IAS Metadata

  1. Open IAS Admin Console → Applications & Resources → Tenant Settings → SAML 2.0 Configuration.
  2. Click Download Metadata File. Save the XML.

13.2 Configure SAML2 in S/4HANA

  1. Run transaction SAML2 in S/4HANA.
  2. Enable SAML 2.0 and create a Local Provider Name.
  3. Trusted Providers tab → Add → Upload Metadata File → upload IAS metadata.
  4. Set NameID format (Login Name recommended — maps to ABAP user ID).
  5. Download S/4HANA SP metadata.

13.3 Register S/4HANA as Application in IAS

  1. IAS Admin Console → Applications → + Add.
  2. Upload S/4HANA SP metadata under Trust → SAML 2.0 Configuration.
  3. Set Subject Name Identifier to match your ABAP user attribute (Login Name).
  4. Save.

Validation: Opening FLP URL redirects to IAS login. After login, FLP opens without ABAP credential prompts. Joule opens without a separate login.

6. Troubleshooting

Symptom

Likely Cause

Fix

Joule icon not visible in FLP

Shell plugin not loaded or role not assigned

Verify role Z_JOULE_S4HANA is assigned. Hard-reload FLP (Ctrl+Shift+R). Check browser console for plugin errors.

'File not found' when Joule loads

Trailing slash in plugin URL

Remove trailing slash: use .../webclient/s4 not .../webclient/s4/.

'Refused to frame/connect' error

Missing Trusted Domain entry

Add the exact blocked origin (shown in browser console) to BTP and IAS Trusted Domains.

Work Zone Channel: Connectivity Error

Real-time callbacks not supported (on-premise)

If status shows 'Updated' with Connectivity Error — content synced. Re-sync manually as needed.

Work Zone 'authorization error' on fetch

Destination URL missing CDM3 path

Ensure destination URL is http://<host>:<port>/sap/bc/ui2/cdm3.

Booster fails: 'System already part of formation'

IAS can only be in one Joule formation

Find the existing Joule formation and add your systems to it instead.

SCC mapping 'Not Reachable'

Firewall blocking the port

Ask network team to open the port between SCC and ABAP server. Try internal IP instead of hostname.

Joule opens but prompts for login

IAS SSO not configured for FLP

Complete Step 13 — configure SAML2 trust between S/4HANA and IAS.

Work Zone destination name error

Name has uppercase or underscores

Rename destination to lowercase with no underscores (e.g., jouletc).

Wrong Joule URL after S/4HANA 2025 upgrade

DAS_WEBCLIENT_PLUGIN set to pilot URL

Update /UI2/FLP_SYS_CONF (SM30) with your tenant URL, or remove the parameter.

7. Summary — What We Achieved

  • Joule provisioned on SAP BTP and linked to IAS and Work Zone via a Formation
  • SAP Cloud Connector established as the secure bridge between BTP and S/4HANA
  • Work Zone Channel Manager syncing S/4HANA Fiori catalogs to BTP
  • BTP Destinations correctly routing Work Zone to S/4HANA via Cloud Connector
  • Joule Shell Plugin configured in S/4HANA FLP via a Technical Catalog and PFCG role
  • Joule icon visible and functional in the S/4HANA Fiori Launchpad

entry.title: Re: Request for SAP Cloud ALM backup and recovery process...
entry.content:
Dear Customer,
 

Thank you for your inquiry. Please find the answers to your How-To questions below:

1. Backups are performed regularly in the data center region where the customer’s SAP Cloud ALM tenant is provisioned.


2. Backup and restore capabilities are in place for SAP Cloud ALM’s persistence platform services, specifically SAP HANA Cloud (SAP HANA DB services) — a Database-as-a-Service (DBaaS) offering managed by SAP BTP. According to the official Backup and Recovery documentation:

SAP HANA Cloud database instances are continuously backed up, ensuring a maximum recovery point objective (RPO) of 15 minutes. For point-in-time recovery, the RPO is typically close to zero, as filled log segments are written to disk every few seconds. In periods of low activity, it may take longer to fill a segment, but it is always written to disk within 15 minutes.


3. For detailed RPO and RTO information for SAP HANA Cloud and SAP BTP, please refer to the following documentation:

SAP HANA Cloud (SAP HANA DBservices):

https://help.sap.com/docs/hana-cloud/sap-hana-cloud-administration-guide/backup-and-recovery

3624301 - RTO and RPO for HANA Cloud 

Database-as-a-service offering (DBaaS) managed by SAP BTP:

https://help.sap.com/docs/btp/sap-business-technology-platform/resilience-high-availability-and-disaster-recovery

 

4. For the disaster recovery, please refer to KBA 3465608.

3465608 - SAP BTP Disaster Recovery - RPO and RTO timelines

 

5. & 6. At this time, restoring a deleted configuration, test case, or user in SAP Cloud ALM is not supported.


7. There is currently no recycle bin functionality available in SAP Cloud ALM.

 

8.To prevent recurrence, restrict delete permissions to appropriate roles and ensure users carefully review the deletion warning before confirming.

 

For more detailed information, please refer to the following resources:
3729180 - Disaster Recovery options for SAP Cloud ALM(CALM)
3741542 - Test case and Execution history deleted by mistake - restore possible?
3081375 - Information about SAP BTP Disaster Recovery

Best regards,

Haterm

SAP Support

entry.title: Shape Intelligent Returns in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud — Join Our Customer Engagement Initiative
entry.content:
Are you working with Customer Returns in the Order-to-Cash area in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition?  We want to hear from you.

 

SAP is launching a Customer Engagement Initiative (CEI) focused on the future of intelligent, automated returns management — and we are inviting customers and partners to help shape it from the start.
 
What is a CEI?

A Customer Engagement Initiative is SAP's way of bringing customers and partners directly into the development process, not after decisions are made, but while they are still being shaped. As a participant, you get a seat at the table: early access to concept ideas, prototypes and solution approaches, with the opportunity to give structured feedback that directly influences the SAP product roadmap.
 
What's This CEI About?

Returns management is one of the most process-intensive areas in Order-to-Cash. From initiating a return order through material inspection, refund determination, and final settlement, every step still relies heavily on manual judgment and repetitive coordination.

This initiative — Intelligent Returns: Process Discovery & Automation Roadmap — will explore exactly where that changes. Through "Day-In-The-Life" sessions with real practitioners, we will map how returns processes actually run today, surface the decisions and data gaps that slow teams down, and identify which tasks are most ready for AI-assisted resolution. We will also share early-stage UX concepts for automated flows and want your honest input on whether they solve real problems.
 
Your insights will go directly into the requirements for SAP's future intelligent returns capabilities.
 
Why Should You Join?
  • Be heard — findings from this CEI will serve as direct input for SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition
  • Influence the roadmap early — your feedback shapes what gets built, not just how it is polished
  • Preview what is coming — including AI use cases and automation concepts before they go public
  • Connect directly with SAP — a direct line to SAP Product Management and UX Research

How to Register?

Registration is open now, and will remain so till June 19, 2026. Estimated effort is approximately 2 hours.

Register here:  Intelligent Returns CEI 

If you are a customer working with returns processes in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition, we would love to have you involved.


#SAPBusinessAI  #Joule  #AIAgents  #CloudERP  #GenerativeAI  #SAPCommunity

  • BTM community (here) grew from 0 to 100+ in last 4 months of 2026 since its inception in January 2026

I will start with myself:

I am Johnson - everyone calls me Johny mostly. I am in SAP from past 20 years of my total 30 years of experience in the area of Learning and Development. I have worked mostly in all area of L&D and currently mainly focused mainly on simulations as mode of learning. Very interested in "In-The-Flow-of Work" learning.  From BTM perspective, I am working closely with Integrated Tool Chain and WalkMe as a main tool for Digital Adoption Platform and WalkMe Learning Arc as learning content production tool. Lastly I am budding vibe coding enthusiast interested in getting on to Agentic AI work. 

I would love to see more interactions and learning happening through this community. 

Please feel free to add your intros and interests.

Regards,

Johny

entry.title: Re: Data Action Function Not Working
entry.content:
@chetan_devrukhkar 

You have not provided your code example to determine if its working or not. Please note This configuration does not imply that all nulls or un booked intersection will be made 0. Its used in the context of source and target range to determine if the source un booked values are used while assigning target.

For example.

Existing Data.

 202512202601
Account110050
Account2 100
Account3200150
Account4300200
Account5 400

If we copy data using below code

DATA("[d/date]="202601")=RESULTLOOKUP("[d/date]="202512")

Config set to OFF : Only booked source range is assigned.

 202512202601
Account1100100
Account2 100
Account3200200
Account4300300
Account5 400

Config set to ON: Un booked range is also used with null treated as 0.

 202512202601
Account1100100
Account2 0
Account3200200
Account4300300
Account5 0

 

Hope this helps !

Nikhil

entry.title: Currency Conversion in SAP ABAP Using CONVERT_TO_LOCAL_CURRENCY with Custom F4 Help
entry.content:
Introduction :

Currency conversion is a common requirement in SAP applications, especially when working with international business transactions. SAP provides the standard function module CONVERT_TO_LOCAL_CURRENCY  to convert an amount from one currency to another using the exchange rates maintained in the system. In this blog, we will create a simple ABAP report that allows users to select source and target currencies using custom F4 help and perform currency conversion based on the latest exchange rate.

Code :

REPORT zva_convert_currency.

*---------------------------------------------------------------------*
* Selection Screen for Input Parameters
*---------------------------------------------------------------------*
SELECTION-SCREEN BEGIN OF BLOCK b1 WITH FRAME TITLE text-001.

  PARAMETERS:
    p_amount TYPE ktwrt,          " Amount to be converted
    p_fcurr  TYPE tcurc-waers,    " Source currency
    p_tcurr  TYPE tcurc-waers.    " Target currency

SELECTION-SCREEN END OF BLOCK b1.

*---------------------------------------------------------------------*
* F4 Help for Source Currency (From Currency)
*---------------------------------------------------------------------*
AT SELECTION-SCREEN ON VALUE-REQUEST FOR p_fcurr.
  " Call reusable F4 help form
  PERFORM get_currency_f4 USING 'P_FCURR'.

*---------------------------------------------------------------------*
* F4 Help for Target Currency (To Currency)
*---------------------------------------------------------------------*
AT SELECTION-SCREEN ON VALUE-REQUEST FOR p_tcurr.
  " Call reusable F4 help form
  PERFORM get_currency_f4 USING 'P_TCURR'.

*---------------------------------------------------------------------*
* Main Processing Logic - Currency Conversion
*---------------------------------------------------------------------*
START-OF-SELECTION.

  " Variable to store converted amount
  DATA: lv_local_amount TYPE ktwrt.

  " Standard FM for currency conversion
  CALL FUNCTION 'CONVERT_TO_LOCAL_CURRENCY'
    EXPORTING
      date             = sy-datum            " Conversion date
      foreign_amount   = p_amount            " Input amount
      foreign_currency = p_fcurr             " Source currency
      local_currency   = p_tcurr             " Target currency
      type_of_rate     = 'M'                 " Exchange rate type (M = average rate)
      read_tcurr       = 'X'                 " Read TCURR table
    IMPORTING
      local_amount     = lv_local_amount     " Converted amount result
    EXCEPTIONS
      no_rate_found    = 1
      overflow         = 2
      no_factors_found = 3
      no_spread_found  = 4
      derived_2_times  = 5
      OTHERS           = 6.

  " Check conversion success
  IF sy-subrc = 0.
    WRITE: / 'Amount           :', p_amount,
           / 'From Currency    :', p_fcurr,
           / 'To Currency      :', p_tcurr,
           / 'Converted Amount :', lv_local_amount.
  ELSE.
    WRITE: / 'Currency conversion failed.'.
  ENDIF.

*---------------------------------------------------------------------*
* Form Routine - F4 Help for Currency Fields
*---------------------------------------------------------------------*
FORM get_currency_f4 USING pv_field.

  " Internal table to hold currency list
  SELECT a~waers,
         b~ltext
    FROM tcurc AS a
    INNER JOIN tcurt AS b
      ON a~waers = b~waers
   WHERE b~spras = @SY-langu
    INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_list).

  " Standard F4 help function module
  CALL FUNCTION 'F4IF_INT_TABLE_VALUE_REQUEST'
    EXPORTING
      retfield    = 'WAERS'        " Return field
      dynpprog    = sy-repid       " Current program
      dynpnr      = sy-dynnr       " Screen number
      dynprofield = pv_field       " Target field on screen
      value_org   = 'S'            " Structured values
    TABLES
      value_tab   = lt_list.

ENDFORM.

Output :

Conclusion :

Using the standard function module CONVERT_TO_LOCAL_CURRENCY , currency conversion can be implemented easily and efficiently in ABAP. By combining it with a custom F4 help for currency selection, users can quickly choose currencies and perform conversions without manual input errors. This approach provides a simple and user-friendly solution for handling currency conversions in SAP applications.

ABAP Development

entry.title: SAPPHIRE Madrid 2026: What Stayed With Me
entry.content:
Madrid was a great show. I want to share what stayed with me.

A small thing first. Orlando had all the big announcements. So Madrid was quieter on the news front. That was actually a good thing for me. It gave us time. Time at booths. Time to talk to people. Time for the kind of chats that don't happen on a busy day.

These are the moments I want to write about.

The SAP Experience Centre

My favourite stop was the SAP Experience Centre.

In our industry, we are very good at talking about what is possible. We have slide decks. We have demos. We have business cases. But we are not always as good at letting a customer inspire what is possible. There is a difference.

A customer who reads a deck might nod and smile. A customer who walks through a real scenario, sees the work, holds the design, leaves the room thinking differently. That is what the Experience Centre did.. not all singing and dancing, but the experience it gives and inspires you with the art of possible is great! 

I had a personal reason to look closely. At Bluestonex, we are part of the SAP AppHaus Network. We are building our own Experience Centre at home. Madrid gave us a clear picture of how good this can be when it is done well. 

If you have not looked into the SAP AppHaus Network, it is worth a look. It is where design and delivery come together. Some of the best customer conversations I have seen happen there.

At the Agent Lab

The Agent Lab was the highlight for me on the technical side. The format was simple. A small space. Real people from the platform team sitting and talking, helping with the hands-on. 

I spent good time with the Joule Studio team. The one piece of news that stopped me was this. n8n is being added to Joule Studio.

I think this is bigger than it sounds. n8n is where a lot of developers and citizen builders already live. Bringing it into Joule Studio is not just about adding a tool. It is a sign that SAP is going to meet builders where they already are. That, to me, is the right move.

People

A few people, but ones that mattered.

The Infosys booth had real thing to show. It was the kind of stand where you wanted to stop, sit, and talk about their work. Whoever set the tone there did a good job.

I also met the Qorelo cofounders. Small world. Sharp team. If you haven't heard about them,  for now, just know they are worth watching.

And one more, which might sound odd. Roger Federer had a session. It was actually really good. He was thoughtful. He was honest. It was not a flashy keynote. It was a quiet one, in the best way.

What didn't quite work

I want to be honest here. That is what is useful to the community.

Many sessions could have been better. Some demos felt too rehearsed. Sometiomes I felt the session titles wre delebrately phrased in such a way that it can attract people. They were so polished it was hard to tell what was the real product and what was just a clever build for the stage. Customer stories were thin in the middle. We heard a lot about where the customer ended up. We did not hear enough about the hard part in the middle. That is the part where the rest of us actually learn.

And here is the one that has stayed with me most. In our industry, we do amazing work that we almost never talk about. SAPPHIRE feels like the right place to fix that. To put the real stories on the stage, even the messy parts. Madrid did not, quite. I hope next year does.

What made it worth it

Big shows like this are rarely about what happens on the main stage. The good stuff happens at the back of a booth, over dinner with people you didn't know you would meet.

Good food. Good company. The kind of small conversations that don't end up in a slide deck. But they do change how you think about your year.

I came home with new ideas. I came home with a few new friends. I came home thinking about my work a bit differently. That is enough for me.

A lovely surprise

A few people came up to me at the venue. They said they watch SAP Content on my YouTube channel for the community. I did not expect that. It made my whole trip feel different. When you make videos, you don't always know who is watching. It was nice to hear hello from a real person. 😍

If that was you, thank you. Truly. That kind of small moment is what makes this community feel real.

The Beginning of Better

The event's tagline was "The Beginning of Better." For a beginning, it was honest. It was ambitious. It was worth showing up for. It was not perfect. But the only way next year can be better is if we are honest about this year.

I would love to know what stayed with you. Drop your moment in the comments. The best part of this community is hearing each other out.

entry.title: Purpose Built Intelligent Agents designed in SAP Joule Studio
entry.content:
AI agents have fundamentally transformed the way the IT industry operates. Beyond well‑recognized benefits—such as automation of complex tasks, increased productivity, scalability, continuous operation, and adaptive, intelligent decision‑making—the human‑in‑the‑loop approach ensures that authorized personnel remain informed, accountable, and actively involved in critical business decisions.

While numerous tools exist for building AI agents, a key challenge today is fragmentation. Agents developed using different tools and technologies often operate in silos, with limited data exchange and interoperability. This significantly restricts their overall effectiveness. Security further compounds this challenge, as agents built on diverse open‑source technologies may not consistently adhere to standardized agentic security and compliance frameworks. As a result, innovation can be constrained, and developers are required to continuously update and strengthen governance models.

SAP Joule Studio addresses these challenges by enabling organizations to deliver secure, scalable, and business‑centric agentic AI experiences tailored to their specific needs. As part of SAP Build, Joule Studio also leverages other SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) services. It integrates SAP AI Foundation for execution‑related components and SAP Integration Suite to govern and manage the APIs consumed by agents and skills. This cohesive architecture allows Joule Studio to stand out by delivering deeply integrated, enterprise‑grade skills and agents aligned with real business outcomes.

With SAP Joule Studio, organizations can design and deploy custom Joule skills and Joule agents tailored to specific business use cases, driving enhanced operational efficiency and innovation across the enterprise.

SAP has published couple of sample agents that can serve as an example in building your own business agents. These are free to use once you have obtained ‘SAP Build-default’ license plan.

Supplier Agent: Supplier Agent help users query supplier information using natural language, and the agent can aggregate data from diverse sources, enabling better and more optimized operational planning.
This sample agent uses 2 databases to retrieve required information.
1: SAP S/4HANA Cloud - Public Edition
2: Northwind Database: A third party free sample database containing data for a fictional food-import/export company, “Northwind Traders.” It Includes tables like Customers, Orders, Products, Employees, Suppliers, etc. We have used an OData API endpoint for customers and suppliers in this same agent. The supplier agent is designed in Joule studio capability of SAP Build and contains the below Joule Skills that are added as tools to the custom agent:
• Retrieve Customer Order Details
- Customer details are retrieved from the Northwind database via the OData API.
- Action project used: 'Northwind Actions'
• Retrieve Food Supplier Details (Northwind)
- Supplier details are retrieved from the Northwind database via the OData API.
- Action project used: 'Northwind Actions'
• Retrieve Technology Supplier Details S/4HANA
- Supplier details are retrieved from the SAP S/4HANA Cloud - Public Edition
- Action project used: 'Business Partner SAP S4HANA (A2X)'.

Procurement Risk Analyzer Agent: The Procurement Risk Analyzer agent helps procurement managers identify potential supplier delivery risks by analyzing open purchase orders and their expected delivery dates.
The agent retrieves open purchase orders from SAP S/4HANA Cloud and evaluates whether deliveries are overdue or at risk of delay. It can summarize late purchase orders, group them by supplier, and highlight suppliers that may cause delivery risks.
This agent is useful for questions about delayed deliveries, overdue purchase orders, supplier delivery reliability, and procurement risks.

Custom agents created by SAP Partners:

Several SAP partners have also developed AI agents, which are available as free content on the SAP Business Accelerator Hub.

1: Master Conversational Agent for Customer Order Processing  - by Infosys:

The Master Conversational Agent orchestrates multiple sub-agents to support sales order lifecycle management through natural language. It enables users to create, modify, track, and resolve sales orders efficiently while enforcing business rules and data consistency. Future SAP BTP integration enables centralized governance, monitoring, and continuous improvement.

2: Material Management and Sales Operations Agent  - by Blueprint Technologies:

The process supports end-to-end material inquiry and sales execution through an intelligent agent. When a user requests material availability, the agent retrieves real-time stock information by invoking the Material Stocks service and presents current quantity and status. If the user asks for alternatives, the agent triggers the Material Determination capability to identify and return valid substitute materials based on system configuration. When the user proceeds to create a sales order, the agent collects any missing mandatory inputs, invokes the Sales Order service to create the order, and confirms successful creation with order details. 

The ‘Material Management and Sales Operations agent’ is designed in Joule studio capability of SAP Build and contains the below Joule Skills that are added as tools to the custom agent: 

  • Material Stocks - Retrieves Materials details for a specified Material from SAP S/4HANA, Public Cloud 
  • Material Substitution - A skill to retrieve material substitution for products from SAP S/4HANA, Public Cloud 
  • Sales Order - A skill to create sales based on customer. /Product details in SAP S/4HANA, Public Cloud

3: SAP EHS Incident Agent  - by Wipro:

The EHS Incident Agent is responsible for creating, categorizing, and displaying safety incidents across plant locations in compliance with established rules. Incidents are classified under three categories: 001 - Incident, 002 - Near Miss and 003 - Safety Observation. Each incident must reference an active EHS Location ID, include a title, and capture key details such as date, time, and description.

Core Value Proposition: Our agent empowers employees to capture first-hand information at the exact moment an incident occurs by enabling the person on the scene to update the system immediately, we eliminate 'memory fade' and ensure the most accurate safety data is recorded directly into SAP.

  • Agent is Integrated with SAP S/4HANA API 
    • API_EHS_REPORT_INCIDENT_SRV
  • Agent uses Joule Skills for retrieving and validating the EHS Incident Location.
    • EHSM_IM_GET: Retrieving EHS Incidents List
    • EHSM Location ID details: EHS Location Details
    • EHSM_IM: Creating EHS Incident for Incident categories 001(Incident), 002(Near Miss) & 003(Safety Observation).

More Partner Agents can be discovered in SAP Business Accelerator Hub.

 SAP Build Solutions in SAP Store: SAP partners have designed and built agents and SAP Build Solutions that are available on the SAP Store and can be downloaded upon payment of the listed price for each offering. One of the most popular Agentic solutions in SAP store built by partner is:

Smarter procurement decisions with pegged requirement analysis – by Motiveminds

SAP Build , Joule Studio , Joule , Agentic AI 

@Archana, @nayak_aj

entry.title: Enhancement in VF11

Hello Everyone,

My requirement is to enhance transaction VF11 during the Save action. I am using user exits SDVFX001 and SDVFX008 for this enhancement. However, I am getting a short dump during include activation. Could you please help me identify the issue and suggest the correct approach.

Additionally, if SDVFX001 and SDVFX008 are not suitable for VF11 save processing, please advise on the appropriate enhancement spot or user exit. 

I am changed SUBC to 'F' from 'I' in table PROGDIR. But still I am facing this issues.

Advance Thanks.

entry.title: Re: Stop requirement transfer when sales order is in approval
entry.content:
Hi @LinglingQian ,

信用敞口是根据销售订单中的确认数量来计算的。按照标准信用管理的功能，当销售订单在信用管控的时候，确认数量会被设置为零。所以这个销售订单没有被计算在信用敞口中。

因为确认数量也直接会影响MRP需求的传递。当确认数量为零的时候，销售订单的需求也不会被传递到MRP。

所以最终取决于您的业务流程是如何的。如果您想要让销售订单在信用敞口中被计算，那么就需要去保证确认数量不为零。

Best regards,

Yong

entry.title: Re: Enhancement in VF11
entry.content:
SDVFX001 and SDVFX008 are user exits tied to billing document transfer to accounting (interface between SD and FI). They are not designed for enhancements during VF11 (Cancel Billing Document) save processing. When you try to activate ZXVVFU08, the system expects a proper include structure, but because the exit is not intended for VF11, the generated program fails.

Having said that, the recommendation is

  • Do not modify PROGDIR — that risks system inconsistency.
  • Remove your code from ZXVVFU08 and instead implement logic in:
    • RV60AFZZ (classic exit include), or
    • BADI_SD_BILLING_CANCEL / BADI_SD_BILLING depending on your release.
  • If you need to run logic only during VF11, add a check for sy-tcode = 'VF11' in your enhancement.

entry.title: SBPA Multiple Approval Levels: Fixed Steps or Reusable Loop? Suggestions please!!
entry.content:
Hi SAP Community,

I would like to get expert guidance on the best-practice approach for modelling a multi-level approval workflow in SAP Build Process Automation (SBPA).

We have a business process where the approval flow may go through around 18 sequential approval levels/personas.

In the current design discussion, there are two possible approaches.

Requirement

Most approval levels are using the same custom UI5 approval form.

The differences between the approval levels are mainly:

  • Current approver / approval level

  • Editable or non-editable fields based on the level

  • Routing to the next approver

  • Visibility of some fields or sections based on role/level

The common approval actions are mostly the same:

  • Approve

  • Reject

  • Send back / rework, if applicable

  • Add comments

Approach 1: Separate Approval Steps in SBPA

In this approach, each approval level is created as a separate approval step in SBPA.

For example:

  • Approval Step 1

  • Approval Step 2

  • Approval Step 3

  • …

  • Approval Step 18

Each step may call the same UI5 form, but the context mapping and routing are maintained separately in SBPA.

The advantage is that the process is visually clear and each approval stage is explicitly visible in the process model.

However, the concern is that this creates many repeated steps, repeated context mappings, and higher maintenance effort. In our case, we are also facing SBPA platform context-related issues because of the large number of repeated steps and mappings the browser is craching while opening the steps in SBPA platform.

Approach 2: Reusable Approval Step with Loop / Level-Based Routing

In this approach, we use a reusable approval step or a smaller number of approval steps.

A local process variable, such as currentApprovalLevel, is used to identify the current level.

The approver is determined dynamically based on the current level using API/master data/ECC OData.

After one approval is completed, the process checks whether another approval level is required. If yes, it moves to the next approval level and reuses the same approval step.

Example:

  1. currentApprovalLevel = 1

  2. Determine Level 1 approver

  3. Create approval task

  4. On approval, increment the approval level

  5. Determine the next approver

  6. Continue until all required approval levels are completed

The advantage is that it avoids repeating the same approval step many times when the UI form and approval behavior are the same.

The concern raised is that this may reduce process visibility and auditability if different personas have different business rules, SLA, rejection paths, escalation logic, or parallel approvals.

Main Question

From an SAP Build Process Automation best-practice perspective, which approach is recommended when many approval levels use the same UI5 form and mostly the same approval behavior?

Should all approval levels be modelled as explicit separate SBPA steps, even if the same form and same approval pattern are repeated?

Or is it acceptable to use a reusable approval step with loop/level-based routing for repeated same-pattern approval levels?

Additional Clarification

We are not trying to move the complete business process logic outside SBPA.

For approval stages that have different behavior, such as different UI, different SLA, different rejection path, different escalation logic, different business rules, or parallel approval, we understand that those should be modelled as separate SBPA steps.

The question is mainly about repeated approval levels where the behavior is the same and only the approver level, field editability, and routing change.

Would a hybrid approach be better, where different business stages are modelled separately, but repeated same-pattern approval levels are handled using reusable approval logic?

I would appreciate your guidance and suggestions based on SAP Build Process Automation best practices.

Thanks in advance.

SAP Build Process Automation SAP Intelligent Robotic Process Automation SAP Advanced Planning and Optimization

entry.title: Re: Querying SAP SuccessFactors in Natural Language with Claude Desktop and the SF-MCP Server
entry.content: Hi @shaktibarath, since SAP is deprecating Basic Authentication for API calls, will the current authentication in the MCP method stop working after 2H 2026?

entry.title: Re: SBPA Multiple Approval Levels: Fixed Steps or Reusable Loop? Suggestions please!!
entry.content: A hybrid approach seems the most sustainable here. When approval behavior, UI, and decision logic remain consistent, a reusable approval step with level based routing can significantly reduce maintenance effort and platform complexity. Separate SBPA steps are better reserved for stages with distinct business rules, SLA requirements, escalations, or parallel approvals.

entry.title: Re: Enhancement in VF11
entry.content: The issue may not be the exit itself but how the include was modified or activated. A dump during activation often indicates syntax errors, missing includes, or transport inconsistencies. I would recommend revalidating the CMOD implementation and running a full syntax check of the enhancement components.

entry.title: Re: Querying SAP SuccessFactors in Natural Language with Claude Desktop and the SF-MCP Server

Instead of constructing OData queries, users express business questions directly. The abstraction layer here is doing meaningful work in reducing cognitive load for SAP and HR tech users.

entry.title: Re: Stock transfer material aging report
entry.content:
Hi MohammedRiyaz,

The sending plant's GR date is not transferred to the receiving plant during a Stock Transfer process. Stock aging at the receiving plant is calculated based on the Goods Receipt posting date at the receiving plant, not on the original receipt date from the sending plant.

I hope this helps.

Best regards,

Thu Mai

entry.title: A Practical Approach to Automating External Parameter Changes in SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
Introduction

As SAP Integration Suite developers, we regularly need to adjust external parameter values throughout a project’s lifecycle. This is more than a good practice: it helps ensure that interfaces remain tenant-agnostic and easier to maintain across environments.

In many projects, these adjustments are only required during transport between environments. In more complex landscapes, however, what appears to be a simple task can quickly become a major operational challenge for any CPI specialist.

 

Example Scenario

Imagine you have built multiple interfaces and your project also includes SAP standard packages such as Cell and Gene Therapy Orchestration (CGTO) or SAP Digital Manufacturing (DM). In addition, SAP Event Mesh is being used as the event broker for CGTO.

Although this setup may appear straightforward, complications often arise at the architectural level. For reasons such as budget constraints or time pressure, the DEV and QA landscapes in this example include not only the standard systems but also hotfix systems dedicated to specific testing activities.

 

 

 

In the DEV scenario described above, SAP Integration Suite acts as the central backbone for all communication flows. While there may be two instances of SAP CGTO, two of SAP S/4HANA, and so on, there is only one SAP Integration Suite tenant responsible for connecting these systems correctly.

Once this landscape is defined, SAP Integration Suite must be adjusted accordingly to ensure that all communication channels function correctly. This becomes especially critical when the hotfix testing window is extremely limited and the parallel system remains offline. These adjustments can involve hundreds or even thousands of configuration values, making it unrealistic to update them manually within such a short period without a significant risk of error.

To illustrate the scale of the challenge, nearly 200 external parameters may be affected in a scenario like this, for example, SAP clients, queue paths, and system endpoints.

 

Solution Overview

A practical way to address this challenge is to leverage SAP Integration Suite itself, more specifically, the integration content available through SAP Business Accelerator Hub.

This approach makes it possible to extract the affected external parameters, maintain them in a spreadsheet, save them as a CSV file, and submit the update through an external tool such as Postman. The effort of preparing the configuration files is done only once; after that, switching between environment-specific configurations can be completed in just a few minutes by uploading the appropriate file.

 

Soft Configurations Framework

The concept is straightforward: this interface provides a controlled way to manage external parameters in SAP Integration Suite through an external client such as Postman.

 The goal is to reduce manual configuration effort across multiple environments while improving traceability and lowering the risk of human error.

 

How the Solution Works

Below are the iFlows included in this solution scope.

Get iFlows

This iFlow retrieves the external parameters of iFlows within the selected SAP Integration Suite packages and returns the result in either JSON or CSV format.

Options:

  • ignorePackages

Because of the large number of packages involved, this option was created to exclude any packages that fall outside the scope of the hotfix, for example, sandbox packages used for testing or other interfaces that are completely unrelated to the hotfix process.

  • returnPackage

If you only need to query a specific package, this option is much simpler, and significantly faster, than downloading every package available on the tenant.

  • Csv

By default, the interface returns the results in JSON format, which is convenient for inspection and integration. For SAP standard packages or large-scale parameter updates, however, CSV is often the most practical option because it allows all parameters to be reviewed and maintained efficiently in Microsoft Excel.

  • csvDelimiter

The user can also select the delimiter to be used in the CSV output.

 

Update Soft Config

This iFlow reads a JSON or CSV payload and updates the corresponding iFlows within the defined scope. It returns a full JSON report in Postman showing the affected external parameters, the previous and updated values, and the response status for each change.

Options:

  • Email

Because a full JSON report of the affected external parameters is generated, the interface can also send this report to a designated email inbox. This is particularly useful for traceability and audit purposes.

  • Deploy

This option allows the affected iflows to have their changes saved or saved and deployed.

 

iFlow Design and Processing Logic

 

Get All iFlows

Bellow are all the steps for this interface development.

Integration Process

  • Headers – Content Modifier: Reads all header parameters sent by a third-party application, such as Postman.
  • Read CSV Header – Groovy Script: Checks whether a CSV file has been requested.
  • Get Packages – Process Call: Invokes the local Get Packages process.

Get Packages

  • Get Packages – Request Reply: Retrieves all packages from SAP Integration Suite for the specified tenant (defined directly in the URL).
  • Read Packages – Groovy Script: Processes the returned payload and performs the following actions:
    • If returnPackage is enabled, filters the payload based on the packages specified in the client request.
    • If ignorePackages is enabled, returns all packages except those specified in the client request.
    • Creates a clean internal JSON structure containing the package ID, Name, and ShortText.
// Part of the script 
// Full script can be found on the link of the bottom of the article

def slim = results.collect { r ->
        [
            Id        : r?.Id ?: "",
            Name      : r?.Name ?: "",
            ShortText : r?.ShortText ?: ""
        ]
}
  • Total Packages – Router: If no packages are returned, the process terminates.
  • Get Integrations – Looping Process Call: Invokes the Get Artifacts from Package process for each package.

 

Get Artifacts from Package

  • Header – Content Modifier: Resets the current iFlow count.
  • Get Actual Package – Groovy Script: Since the next step requires the package ID for the request, this script retrieves the current package ID from the internal JSON message.
  • Get Artifacts from Package – Request Reply: Retrieves all iFlows associated with the selected package ID.
  • Get iFlows – Groovy Script: Processes the returned payload and appends the iFlow ID, Name, and ShortText, along with the current package ID, to the internal JSON message.
// Part of the script 
// Full script can be found on the link of the bottom of the article

found = false

jsonAll.each { item ->

	if ((item instanceof Map) && String.valueOf(item.Id) == targetPackage) 
	{
		item.Iflows = slim
		found = true
	}
}
  • Empty? – Router: If no iFlows are returned, the process ends and proceeds to the next package.
  • Get Soft Configurations – Looping Process Call: Invokes the local Get Soft Configurations process.

 

Get Soft configurations

  • Get iFlow – Groovy Script: For the package currently in scope, retrieves each iFlow ID required to request its soft configurations in the next step.
// Part of the script 
// Full script can be found on the link of the bottom of the article

if(resultValue)
{      
	message.setProperty("iflowID", resultValue)
	message.setProperty("currentCountIflow", targetIflowIx + 1)
}
  • Get iFlow Soft Configs – Request Reply: Retrieves all soft configurations for each iFlow.
  • Get Soft Configs and Update – Groovy Script: Processes the retrieved information by cleaning the message, filtering out soft configurations defined directly within the iFlow, and appending the remaining entries to the internal JSON message.
// Part of the script 
// Full script can be found on the link of the bottom of the article

Message processData(Message message) {

    def body       = message.getBody(String) ?: '{}'
    def parsed     = new JsonSlurper().parseText(body)
    def results    = parsed?.d?.results ?: []

    def slim = results.collect {
        [
            Parameter   : it?.ParameterKey   ?: "",
            Value       : it?.ParameterValue ?: ""
            //Description : it?.Description    ?: ""
        ]
    }

    ...

    def pkg = jsonAll.find { it?.Id == packageID }

    // Ignore list
    def ignoreList = ignore.split(",")
                        .collect { it.trim() }
                        .findAll { it }

    // remove from list
    slim = slim.findAll { r -> !(r?.Parameter in ignoreList) }    

    if (pkg?.Iflows) {
        // Find the iflow
        def flow = pkg.Iflows.find { it?.IdIflow == iflowID }
        if (flow) {
            flow.SoftConfig = slim
        }
    }
...
}

 

  Integration Process

  • Return Message – Content Modifier: Loads the internal JSON message into the message body.
  • isCSV? – Router: Checks whether a CSV output was requested. If not, the process ends and returns the internal JSON message.
  • CSV Convert – Groovy Script: Converts the JSON message into CSV format using the specified delimiter.
// Part of the script 
// Full script can be found on the link of the bottom of the article

// HEADER
    StringBuilder csv = new StringBuilder()
    csv.append(["PackageID","PackageName","IdIflow","NameIflow","Parameter","Value"].join(separator))
       .append(nl)

    // Clear escapes
    def esc = { v ->
        if (v == null) return ""
        def s = v.toString()
        def needsQuotes = s.contains(separator) || s.contains('"') || s.contains('\n') || s.contains('\r')
        s = s.replace('"', '""')
        needsQuotes ? "\"${s}\"" : s
    }

    // packages
    json.each { pkg ->
        def pkgId   = pkg.Id
        def pkgName = pkg.Name
        def iflows  = pkg.Iflows ?: []

        // iFlows
        iflows.each { iflow ->
            def iflowId   = iflow.IdIflow
            def iflowName = iflow.NameIflow
            def softCfgs  = iflow.SoftConfig ?: []

            // SoftConfig
            softCfgs.each { cfg ->
                def param = cfg.Parameter
                def value = cfg.Value

                csv.append([
                    esc(pkgId),
                    esc(pkgName),
                    esc(iflowId),
                    esc(iflowName),
                    esc(param),
                    esc(value)
                ].join(separator)).append(nl)
            }
        }
    }
  • Header – Content Modifier: Updates the Content-Type header to text/csv.
  • End: Terminates the process.

 

Update Soft Configurations

Bellow are all the steps for this interface development.

Integration Process

  • Count – Content Modifier: Reads all configurations and header parameters sent by a third-party application, such as Postman.
  • Validate JSON Message – Groovy Script: Checks whether the incoming message is in JSON or CSV format, validates its structure, and transforms the payload into an internal JSON message.
// Part of the script 
// Full script can be found on the link of the bottom of the article

Message processData(Message message) {

    ...
        
    // is JSON?
    try {
        parsed = slurper.parseText(body)
        if (parsed instanceof Map || parsed instanceof List) {
            isJson = true
        }
    } catch (Exception e) {
        isJson = false
    }

    // if its json, end
    if (!isJson) 
    {
        ...
    }
    else
    {
        /////////////////////////////////////////
        //json
        /////////////////////////////////////////
        
        try 
        {
            json = new JsonSlurper().parseText(body)
        } catch (e) {
            error = "Invalid JSON format."
        }

        // array?
        if (!(json instanceof List)) {
            error = "JSON must be an array at root level."
        }

        json.eachWithIndex { item, idx ->

            // IdIflow
            if (!item?.IdIflow || !(item.IdIflow instanceof String)) {
                error = "Element at index ${idx} missing valid 'IdIflow' (string)."
            }

            // SoftConfig
            if (!(item?.SoftConfig instanceof List)) {
                error = "Element '${item.IdIflow}' missing valid 'SoftConfig' array."
            }

            // config
            item.SoftConfig.eachWithIndex { cfg, cidx ->
                if (!cfg?.Parameter || !(cfg.Parameter instanceof String)) {
                    error = "Invalid 'Parameter' in '${item.IdIflow}' at SoftConfig index ${cidx}."
                }
                if (cfg.Value == null) { // allowed empty, but not null
                    error = "Missing 'Value' in '${item.IdIflow}' at SoftConfig index ${cidx}."
                }
            }
        }
    }
    def deploy = properties.get("deploy")
        
    if(!deploy)
    {
        message.setProperty("deploy", 'NO')
    }
    else
    {
        if( deploy.toUpperCase() != 'YES')
            message.setProperty("deploy", 'NO')
        else
            message.setProperty("deploy", 'YES')        
    }
...
}

 

  • All Good? – Router: Verifies whether the message is valid. If validation fails, the process ends and returns an error message.
  • iFlow – Looping Process Call: Invokes the local iFlow Logic process.

 

Iflow Logic

  • Get iFlow – Groovy Script: Reads information for each iFlow from the internal payload.
  • Get SoftConfigs – Request Reply: Retrieves the existing soft configurations for the iFlow currently in scope.
  • Store Previous Configs – Content Modifier: Stores the response payload for later use.
  • Fetch Token – Process Call: Invokes the Fetch Token local process.

 

Fetch Token

  • Header – Content Modifier: Configures the request to fetch the X-CSRF token header required for the next call.
  • Fetch Token – Request Reply: Retrieves the token through a GET request.
  • End: Terminates the local process and returns control to the iFlow Logic process call. 

 

 Iflow Logic

  • Soft Configs – Looping Process Call: Iteratively invokes the local Soft Configs process for each configuration item.

 

Soft Configs

  • Get Soft Config – Groovy Script: Processes each soft parameter individually for the iFlow currently in scope.
// Part of the script 
// Full script can be found on the link of the bottom of the article

if (configIndex < 0 || configIndex >= obj.SoftConfig.size()) 
{
   message.setProperty("currentParameter", "")
   message.setProperty("currentParameterValue", "")
}
else
{
   def value     = obj.SoftConfig[configIndex]?.Value?.toString()
   def parameter = obj.SoftConfig[configIndex]?.Parameter?.toString()

   message.setProperty("currentParameterValue", value)
   message.setProperty("currentParameter", parameter)
}
  • Prepare Body – Content Modifier: Configures the request body in the expected format for the next call and sets the required CSRF token headers.
  • Update Config – Request Reply: Updates the current soft parameter through a PUT request.
  • Read Response – Groovy Script: Reads the HTTP response code and message, while also retrieving the previous value that was cached earlier in the process.
// Part of the script 
// Full script can be found on the link of the bottom of the article

Message processData(Message message) {

    def body         = message.getBody(String)
    def jsonPrevious = message.getProperty("jsonPrevious")?.toString()
    def parameterKey = message.getProperty("currentParameter")?.toString()
    def head         = message.getHeaders();
    def httpCode     = head.get("CamelHttpResponseCode");

    def json         = new JsonSlurper().parseText(jsonPrevious)

    def results      = json?.d?.results
    def node         = results.find { it?.ParameterKey == parameterKey }
    def value        = node?.ParameterValue?.toString()

    message.setProperty("beforeParameterValue", value)
    message.setProperty("responseMessage", body)
    message.setProperty("responseCode", httpCode)

    return message
}
  • JSON Final Message – Groovy Script: Updates the internal JSON message with the new values obtained from the previous step.
// Part of the script 
// Full script can be found on the link of the bottom of the article

def newCfg = [
        Parameter      : parameter,
        ValueBefore    : valueBefore,
        ValueUpdated   : valueUpdated,
        ResponseCode   : responseCode,
        ResponseMessage: responseMsg
]

def existingIdx = node.SoftConfig.findIndexOf { it?.Parameter == parameter }

if (existingIdx >= 0) 
{
   node.SoftConfig[existingIdx] = newCfg
} 
else 
{
   node.SoftConfig << newCfg
}
  • End: Once the loop finishes, control returns to the iFlow Logic process and execution continues.

 

iFlow Logic

  • Deploy? – Router: Checks whether deployment was requested by the user.
  • Deploy – Request Reply: Sends a POST request using the iFlow ID currently in scope to trigger deployment.
  • Update JSON – Groovy Script: Updates the internal JSON message with the deployment result.
  • End: If no additional iFlows remain, control returns to the Integration Process.

 

Integration Process

  • Final Payload – Content Modifier: Loads the internal JSON message into the message body.
  • Monitor – Groovy Script: Attaches the payload to the MPL.
// Part of the script 
// Full script can be found on the link of the bottom of the article

if (messageLog != null) 
{
   messageLog.addAttachmentAsString('Soft config report', body, 'text/plain')
}
  • Has Email – Router: Checks whether an email request was provided. If not, the process ends.
  • Send Email – Send: Sends an email with the payload attached.

Applicability

 

Interface Connection Management

This was the primary motivation for creating the interface. Even with strong controls in place, manual configuration always carries some risk. Storing configurations in separate files not only saves time, but also provides a more reliable and resilient operating model. Teams can maintain environment-specific configurations independently and apply them when needed.

 

Technical Documentation

Documenting external parameters and their values is rarely a pleasant task. With this interface, parameters can be exported as a CSV file, refined in Microsoft Excel, and then reused in technical documentation with far less manual effort.

 

Configurations After Transport to Another Environment

From a governance perspective, managing critical parameters in a controlled format can be preferable to maintaining them directly in a web interface. CSV files reduce the risk of mistakes, especially in scenarios where SAP CTMS transports interfaces with incorrect external parameters.

They also make it easier to identify patterns and provide a broader, more structured view of the configuration landscape.

 

Final thoughts

I created this tool to address a highly time-consuming challenge in day-to-day delivery work. As a result, it was designed with enough flexibility to evolve and support additional use cases over time. Potential enhancements could include AI-based support for technical documentation or simplified access for non-technical colleagues and clients who need a clearer view of active integrations.

Unfortunately, time, or more precisely, the lack of it, has been the main constraint preventing further development of these additional capabilities.

I hope you find this tool as useful as I found the process of creating it. There are, of course, many ways to solve the same problem; this is simply one approach that I enjoyed designing and implementing.
